redFlags: Sender domain is hdfcbank-secure.com — not hdfcbank.com
Urgent language: suspended within 24 hours
Link goes to hdfc-verify.ru — .ru TLD unrelated to HDFC
Generic greeting: Dear Customer instead of your name
Threatens account suspension to force immediate action
explanation: This is a phishing email. HDFC Bank's real domain is hdfcbank.com — any variation like hdfcbank-secure.com is fake. The .ru destination link is a dead giveaway. Real banks never threaten suspension via email.
educationTip: Always hover over links before clicking. If the destination domain doesn't exactly match the company's official website, don't click.

data.subject: URGENT: Your HDFC Bank account will be suspended
data.body:
Dear Customer,

We have detected unusual login activity on your HDFC Bank account. To prevent unauthorized access, your account will be suspended within 24 hours unless you verify your identity immediately.

Click the link below to complete verification and restore full access.

Failure to verify will result in permanent account suspension.

HDFC Bank Security Team

redFlags: IRCTC never sends refunds via SMS with a link
Real IRCTC refunds go directly to your original payment method
URL is irctc-refund.in — not irctc.co.in
Creates urgency: expires in 2 hours
Asks you to claim money — banks don't work this way
explanation: IRCTC refunds are automatically credited to your original payment method — they never ask you to click a link to claim a refund. The domain irctc-refund.in has no connection to Indian Railways.
educationTip: No legitimate company will ever ask you to click a link to receive a refund. Refunds are always pushed to you automatically.

data.body: IRCTC: Your ticket refund of Rs.847 is ready. Claim your refund before it expires in 2 hours. Visit http://irctc-refund.in/claim?ref=TXN8847291 - Indian Railways

redFlags: Domain is google-accounts-signin.com — not accounts.google.com
Legitimate Google sign-in is always on accounts.google.com
Subdomain trick: accounts appears in the URL but is not the actual domain
HTTP not HTTPS
explanation: This URL uses accounts as a subdomain to look like Google, but the actual domain is google-accounts-signin.com — a fake site. Google's real sign-in URL is always accounts.google.com.
educationTip: Read URLs from right to left, stopping at the first slash. Everything before that slash is the domain. accounts.google-accounts-signin.com is owned by google-accounts-signin.com, not Google.

data.context: You received this link in an email saying your Google account was accessed from a new device

redFlags: Sender email is officially from netflix.com domain
Link points directly to the real https://www.netflix.com
No threatening language or urgent ultimatums
explanation: This is a legitimate password reset email from Netflix. It comes from the correct domain and points to the official website.
educationTip: Legitimate password resets will only occur if you requested them. If you get one unexpectedly, don't click it, but the email itself isn't necessarily malicious.

data.subject: Reset your password
data.body:
Hi,

We received a request to reset the password for your Netflix account.

If you requested this, click the link below to set a new password.

If you didn't make this request, you can safely ignore this email.

redFlags: Generic greeting 'Dear User'
Domain is suspicious 'it-support-portal-online.com' rather than company domain
Urgent ultimatum to prevent receiving emails
explanation: Fake IT helpdesk emails are common. Real IT departments will email you from an internal company address, not a generic online portal.
educationTip: Always verify internal IT emails by checking the exact sender address. When in doubt, message IT on Slack or Teams.

data.subject: URGENT: Mailbox Quota Exceeded
data.body:
Dear User,

Your mailbox has exceeded its storage limit. You will not be able to send or receive new messages until you upgrade your quota.

Click below to upgrade your quota for free.

Thank you,
IT Dept

redFlags: Creates panic with a large unauthorized charge
Link domain is not paypal.com
Encourages clicking a link to cancel an order instead of logging into the app
explanation: This email creates artificial panic to make you click a phishing link. Real PayPal emails come from paypal.com, not paypal-receipts-update.com.
educationTip: Never click 'Cancel' links in receipt emails you don't recognize. Open a new browser tab, go to the official website, and check your account activity.

data.subject: Receipt for your payment to Coinbase
data.body:
Hello,

You sent a payment of $499.00 USD to Coinbase.

If you did not authorize this charge, please click the link below to cancel the transaction and claim a refund immediately.

PayPal Support

redFlags: Domain exactly matches apple.com
Uses HTTPS
No suspicious subdomains or typos
explanation: This is the real Apple ID login portal. The domain ends exactly in apple.com.
educationTip: Always verify the root domain (the word right before the .com or .org). Here it is apple, which is correct.

data.context: You are trying to manage your Apple subscriptions on a new laptop

redFlags: Domain is instagram-login-support.com, not instagram.com
Missing HTTPS (not secure)
Suspicious context to force a login
explanation: This URL is attempting to steal Instagram credentials. The domain is not owned by Instagram.
educationTip: Legitimate tech companies will never use hyphenated 'support' or 'login' domains for their main authentication pages.

data.context: Received via SMS claiming your account is about to be disabled

redFlags: No links to click
Informational only, no action required
Standard bank SMS format with masked account number
explanation: This is a standard SMS notification for a bank deposit. It doesn't ask you to do anything or click anywhere.
educationTip: Safe messages simply inform you of events. Messages that require you to click a link to 'verify' or 'claim' are almost always scams.

data.body: Dear Customer, your account XX1234 has been credited with Rs 500.00 on 12-May. Avl Bal: Rs 1500.00. -SBI

redFlags: Sent from a personal 10-digit mobile number, not an official shortcode
Creates extreme urgency (disconnect tonight)
Terrible grammar and formatting
explanation: This is a classic scam in India. Electricity boards do not send disconnection threats from personal 10-digit mobile numbers.
educationTip: Official communications will come from registered Sender IDs (like AD-BESCOM), not random mobile phone numbers.

data.body: Dear Customer, your electricity power will be disconnected tonight at 9:30 PM from the electricity office because your previous month bill was not updated. Please call our electricity officer at 9876543210 immediately.

redFlags: Sender email is orders@amazon-delivery-india.com — not amazon.in
Order number format doesn't match real Amazon
Link domain is amazon-order-verify.com — not amazon.in
Asks to confirm shipping address by clicking — Amazon never does this post-order
Amount is suspiciously round: 4,999
explanation: Amazon's legitimate emails come from amazon.in or amazon.com. The sender domain here is amazon-delivery-india.com — a completely separate site with no link to Amazon.
educationTip: When in doubt about an order, go directly to amazon.in and check My Orders — never click links in emails.

data.subject: Order Confirmation — Your order IN-2024-88471 has been placed
data.body:
Hello,

Thank you for shopping with Amazon India! Your order IN-2024-88471 for Rs.4,999 has been received and is being processed.

Expected delivery: 2-4 business days.

To ensure successful delivery, please confirm your shipping address by clicking the link below. Orders with unconfirmed addresses may be delayed or cancelled.

Amazon Customer Service

redFlags: Zepto never asks you to share your OTP
Legitimate OTP messages always say DO NOT share this OTP with anyone
This SMS asks you to forward the OTP — a social engineering red flag
Fake delivery agent scenario to create a believable reason to share OTP
explanation: This is a smishing attack that tricks you into sharing your OTP under the guise of delivery verification. Real delivery apps like Zepto never require you to share an OTP with a delivery agent.
educationTip: An OTP is a secret between you and the service. No employee, delivery agent, or support person from any legitimate company will ever ask for your OTP.

data.body: Hi, this is Ravi from Zepto delivery. Your order is outside but the app is showing an error. Please share the OTP you just received on this number so I can mark your delivery complete. Sorry for the trouble!

redFlags: Domain is payva1t.com — uses digit 1 instead of letter l (typosquatting)
HTTPS is present — but HTTPS only means the connection is encrypted, NOT that the site is legitimate
Path /securelogin looks convincing but means nothing
Subtle character substitution is a classic hard-to-spot phishing technique
explanation: This URL uses typosquatting — replacing the letter l with the digit 1 in payvault. The site has HTTPS, which tricks many people into thinking it's safe. HTTPS only means your connection to the fake site is encrypted.
educationTip: HTTPS does not mean a website is safe. It only means your connection is encrypted. Scammers can and do get HTTPS certificates for fake sites.

data.context: You received this URL in a WhatsApp message from an unknown number saying your PayVault account shows suspicious activity

redFlags: Sender domain is exactly github.com
Link points directly to github.com
No urgent threats to suspend account
explanation: This is a legitimate GitHub security email. The sender domain is github.com, the link goes to github.com, it addresses you by username, uses no urgency tactics, and contains no threats.
educationTip: Not every security email is a phish. Legitimate ones address you by name/username, come from the real domain, and don't threaten you — they just inform you.

data.subject: New sign-in to your GitHub account
data.body:
Hi vijaydev,

A new sign-in to your GitHub account was detected.

Chrome on Windows — Mumbai, India — Today at 2:34 PM IST

If this was you, no action is needed.

If you don't recognize this sign-in, please secure your account immediately by visiting your account security settings.

The GitHub Team

redFlags: Domain is microsoft-support.net, not microsoft.com
Offers to 'keep current password' which violates real security policies
Urgency factor of 24 hours
explanation: Microsoft does not use domains like microsoft-support.net for Office 365 alerts. Also, if a password policy requires a change, they don't offer a button to 'keep your current password'.
educationTip: Scammers often create domains containing the name of a real company plus words like 'support', 'secure', or 'update'.

data.subject: Action Required: Password expiring in 24 hours
data.body:
Hello,

Your Office 365 password is set to expire in 24 hours according to your organization's security policy.

You can keep your current password by validating your sign-in details below.

Microsoft Security Team

redFlags: Sender email is from the official linkedin.com domain
Link points to genuine www.linkedin.com
Standard formatting for LinkedIn connection requests without urgency
explanation: This is a genuine notification from LinkedIn. It comes from the correct domain and links to the official website.
educationTip: If you're ever unsure about an email notification, log directly into the app (like LinkedIn) and check your notifications there.

data.subject: John Doe would like to connect on LinkedIn
data.body:
Hi,

John Doe (Software Engineer at TechCorp) has sent you a connection request on LinkedIn.

View John's profile to see if you know them.

redFlags: Domain ends in amazon.com
Subdomain console.aws is the standard AWS login path
Uses HTTPS encryption
explanation: This is the real AWS Console URL. It correctly ends in amazon.com.
educationTip: For complex subdomains, always look at the two words right before the first single slash. aws.amazon.com means it's an amazon.com site.

data.context: You typed this into your browser to check your server logs

redFlags: Uses a zero (0) instead of 'o' in dropbox
Domain ends in -share.com instead of just dropbox.com
Classic typosquatting technique
explanation: This is a typosquatting URL designed to look like Dropbox. It uses a zero instead of an 'o' and adds '-share' to the domain.
educationTip: Scammers rely on you reading URLs quickly. Always double-check the spelling of the domain, especially looking for numbers replacing letters.

data.context: A client emailed you a link to an invoice

redFlags: Sender ID is official VM-SWIGGY
Link uses official swiggy.in short domain
Expected message after placing an order
explanation: This is a legitimate Swiggy tracking SMS. It uses their official shortlink domain swiggy.in.
educationTip: Legitimate tracking links should always use the company's official domain or known shortlink.

data.body: Swiggy: Your order from Meghana Foods is out for delivery. Track your order here: https://swiggy.in/track/xyz123

redFlags: Domain is fake (real is incometax.gov.in)
Government departments do not ask for bank details via SMS link
Exploits greed/hope by promising money
explanation: The Income Tax Department never sends links via SMS asking you to verify your bank account. The domain incometax-refund-gov.in is fake.
educationTip: Any unexpected message promising money is likely a scam. Go to the official government portal directly instead of clicking links.

data.body: Important: Your income tax refund of Rs 15,400 has been approved. Please verify your bank account details here to receive funds: https://incometax-refund-gov.in/verify

redFlags: UIDAI's official domain is uidai.gov.in — sender is kyc-update@uidai.gov.in.secure-kyc.com
gov.in appears as a subdomain — real domain is secure-kyc.com
UIDAI never sends KYC requests via email
Deadline pressure: 72 hours or services will be suspended
Very convincing formatting and official-sounding language
explanation: This is a highly convincing phish. The sender email contains uidai.gov.in which looks official, but it appears as part of a subdomain — the actual domain is secure-kyc.com. UIDAI never contacts you via email for KYC updates.
educationTip: When an email address contains a legitimate government domain, look at what comes AFTER the last dot before the @ symbol — that's the real domain. kyc-update@uidai.gov.in.secure-kyc.com is owned by secure-kyc.com, not UIDAI.

data.subject: Action Required: Complete your Aadhaar KYC update before 30th November
data.body:
Dear Aadhaar Holder,

As part of the annual Aadhaar data verification drive mandated by the Government of India, all Aadhaar cardholders are required to complete a digital KYC re-verification by 30th November 2024.

Failure to complete this process within 72 hours will result in temporary suspension of your Aadhaar-linked services including:
- UPI and banking services
- Mobile SIM (TRAI mandate)
- DigiLocker access

The process takes less than 2 minutes. You will need your Aadhaar number and registered mobile phone.

Reference: UIDAI-KYC-2024-MH-771234
Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology, Government of India

redFlags: Domain is s1ack.com — digit 1 replaces letter l, very hard to spot in some fonts
HTTPS present and path looks completely legitimate
No other obvious signs — this is a pure typosquat attack
In certain fonts (Arial, Calibri) 1 and l are visually identical
explanation: This is an extremely subtle typosquatting attack. s1ack.com uses the digit 1 instead of l, making it look identical to slack.com in many fonts. There are no other red flags — this is why it's rated hard.
educationTip: For services you use daily, bookmark the official URL and always access them through bookmarks — never from links in messages or emails.

data.context: A colleague sent this Slack link in a WhatsApp message saying 'check this thread, tagged you'

redFlags: Sender domain is infosys-hr.com — real Infosys HR emails come from infosys.com only
Legitimate offer letters are sent via official portals, not raw email attachments
Asks for PAN and Aadhaar via email — this is never done legitimately
No mention of recruiter employee ID or verifiable contact at official Infosys numbers
Urgency: accept within 48 hours
explanation: Job offer phishing targets freshers and job seekers. Infosys HR will only contact candidates from infosys.com domains and through their official hiring portals. Any request to submit Aadhaar or PAN via email is a data theft attempt.
educationTip: Verify any job offer by calling the company's official number listed on their website — not any number provided in the email. Real HR teams expect you to verify.

data.subject: Congratulations! Offer Letter — Software Engineer, Infosys Limited
data.body:
Dear Candidate,

After careful evaluation of your profile and interviews, we are pleased to extend an offer of employment at Infosys Limited.

Role: Software Engineer / Systems Engineer
Location: Bangalore / Hyderabad
CTC: Rs.3,60,000 per annum
Joining Date: January 15, 2025

Please find your official offer letter attached (PDF).

To confirm your acceptance, please reply to this email with the following documents within 48 hours:
1. Aadhaar Card (front and back)
2. PAN Card
3. 10th, 12th, and degree marksheets
4. Passport size photograph

Offer lapses if not accepted within 48 hours.

Infosys Recruitment Team

redFlags: Sender domain is google-docs.com, which is fake. Real Google Docs come from google.com
Link points to google-docs.com instead of docs.google.com
Highly realistic format mimicking real Google Drive emails
explanation: This is a sophisticated attack using a very realistic domain name. Google does not use 'google-docs.com'. Their services always live on subdomains of google.com.
educationTip: Pay close attention to domains for major tech companies. Scammers often register hyphenated versions of their names.

data.subject: Document shared with you: 'Q4 Financial Projections'
data.body:
Sarah Connor has invited you to view the following document:

Q4 Financial Projections.pdf

Click below to open the document securely.

redFlags: Sender domain accountprotection.microsoft.com is a legitimate Microsoft security domain
Link points to account.live.com, a real Microsoft authentication domain
Does not threaten immediate account suspension, just advises review
explanation: This is a genuine Microsoft security alert. Microsoft actually uses the domain accountprotection.microsoft.com for these alerts, which can look suspicious but is real.
educationTip: If you receive an alert like this and aren't sure, don't click the link. Open your browser, go to microsoft.com, log in, and check your security settings directly.

data.subject: Microsoft account unusual sign-in activity
data.body:
We detected something unusual about a recent sign-in to the Microsoft account.

Sign-in details
Country/region: Russia
IP address: 103.24.11.9
Date: Today

If this was you, you can safely ignore this email.
If you're not sure this was you, a malicious user might have your password. Please review your recent activity and we'll help you secure your account.

redFlags: Domain ends in .co instead of .com
Search ads are frequently hijacked by phishing campaigns
Everything else looks identical to the real URL
explanation: This is an extremely dangerous attack where scammers buy Google Ads to appear at the top of search results. The domain binance.co is fake.
educationTip: Never click on 'Sponsored' ad links when searching for crypto exchanges or banks. Always type the URL manually or use a bookmark.

data.context: You searched 'Binance login' on Google and clicked the first ad result

redFlags: Domain is exactly salesforce.com
HTTPS is active
No subtle misspellings or weird subdomains
explanation: This is a standard Salesforce login URL. It is perfectly safe.
educationTip: A clean URL pointing exactly to the expected root domain (salesforce.com) is a strong indicator of legitimacy.

data.context: You are logging in to work to check your CRM dashboard

redFlags: Uses a URL shortener (bit.ly) to hide the real destination
Asks for a small fee, which is a classic credit card harvesting technique
Legitimate couriers don't charge redelivery fees via SMS
explanation: This scam tricks you into paying a tiny 'redelivery fee'. When you enter your card details on the fake site, they steal the card info and make much larger unauthorized charges.
educationTip: Never pay unexpected small fees via SMS links. If a package is genuinely delayed, check the tracking number on the official website.

data.body: BlueDart: We attempted to deliver your package (AWB 8839210) but no one was available. Please reschedule and pay the Rs 15 redelivery fee: https://bit.ly/bd-redeliver

redFlags: Domain wa-device-security.com is fake
Creates panic by implying your account is compromised
WhatsApp secures accounts within the app, not via external web links
explanation: This SMS creates immense panic. The domain looks plausible but is not owned by WhatsApp.
educationTip: WhatsApp alerts you about linked devices inside the app itself, not via SMS links. Never click external links to manage WhatsApp security.

data.body: WhatsApp: A new device was linked to your account. If this wasn't you, secure your account immediately at https://wa-device-security.com/revoke

redFlags: No links provided
Standard government SMS sender ID
Contains a time-limited OTP with no suspicious instructions
explanation: This is a legitimate OTP message from the CoWIN portal. It contains no links and simply provides the code you requested.
educationTip: Real OTP messages just give you the code and tell you not to share it. They don't include links to 'fix problems' or 'verify accounts'.

data.body: Your OTP to register/access CoWIN is 849201. It will be valid for 3 minutes. - MoHFW
